← World Gallery
Regions Artists Timeline All works

Egypt & Nubia

Three thousand years along the Nile.

Pharaonic Egypt and its southern rival Kush left monuments and portraits of startling precision. The pyramids alone span a thousand years — and Nubia built more of them than Egypt did.

Click any image to enlarge. Use ← → or close with Esc.

Before 1000 BCE

Hand Axe
Egypt, Haraga, excavated in 1914, Predynastic (5000–2950 BCE) · 5000–3000 BCE · tan-colored flint

Egypt, Predynastic (5000–2950 BCE), Naqada I–IIb (3900–3300 BCE) · 4500–3000 BCE · Nile silt pottery

Egypt, Predynastic (5000–2950 BCE), Naqada Ia–b (3900–3500 BCE) · 4500–3500 BCE · light brown flint
Vessel with Lug Handles
by Egyptian
Egypt · old kingdom · -4000–-2250 · Stone
Flint in the Form of a Bird
Egypt, Predynastic (5000–2950 BCE), Naqada II–III (3650–3000 BCE) · 4000–3000 BCE · tan-colored flint
White Cross-Lined Bowl with Turtle and Sun
Egypt, Predynastic (5000–2950 BCE), Naqada I–II (3900–3300 BCE) · c. 4000–3400 BCE · Nile silt pottery
Wavy-Lined Jar
Egypt, Predynastic (5000–2950 BCE), Naqada III (3200–3000 BCE) · 4000–3000 BCE · marl clay pottery
Black-Topped Bowl
Egypt, Predynastic (5000–2950 BCE), Naqada II (3650–3300 BCE) · 4000–3000 BCE · Nile silt
Bull's-Head Amulet
Predynastic · c. 3500 BCE
A tiny stone amulet shaped as a bull's head — pierced to string around the neck. Bull imagery precedes Egypt itself; Narmer's palette is ringed by heads like this.
Predynastic Fish Palette
Predynastic · c. 3500 BCE
A schist cosmetic palette shaped as a fish — used to grind kohl for eye makeup. Predynastic palettes began with animal shapes; a thousand years later they became the ceremonial Narmer-style plaque.
Palette in the Form of a Turtle
Egypt, Predynastic (5000–2950 BCE), Naqada IIa–IIIa (3650–3000 BCE) · c. 3500–3050 BCE · graywacke
Palette
Egypt · c.3500 BC - c.3200 BC
Jar with Painted Decoration
by Egyptian
Egypt · predynastic · -3500–-3200 · Ceramic and pigment
Jar with Handles
by Egyptian
Egypt · predynastic · -3500–-3200 · Breccia
Squat Jar with Lug Handles
Egypt, Predynastic (5000–2950 BCE), Naqada IIb (3650–3300 BCE) · c. 3400–3300 BCE · marl clay pottery
Decorated Jar with Boat Scenes
Egypt, Predynastic (5000–2950 BCE), Naqada IIc–d (3650–3300 BCE) · c. 3300–3100 BCE · marl clay pottery
Gebel el-Arak Knife
Predynastic · c. 3300–3200 BCE
Flint blade with an ivory handle carved in Mesopotamian style — lions, bulls, a robed "master of animals." Evidence of Uruk-period trade reaching the Nile before Egypt was Egypt.
Two-Dog Palette
Predynastic · c. 3200 BCE
A schist palette carved with two tall dogs gripping its edges, a wild hunt scene between them. The prey — gazelle, hare, even a flute-playing fox — bleed into chaos.
Narmer Palette
Early Dynastic · c. 3100 BCE
A ceremonial siltstone palette showing a king smiting an enemy — one of the earliest monuments of unified Egypt, with its conventions already fully formed.
Memphis (Egypt)
Early Dynastic onward · c. 3100 BCE · Lower Egypt
The first capital of unified Egypt, sacred to Ptah. What survives today is ruins in a palm grove — a colossal reclining statue of Ramesses II, sphinxes, embalmed Apis bulls.
Battlefield Palette
Predynastic · c. 3100 BCE
A ceremonial cosmetic palette carved with naked dead enemies being picked over by crows and lions. The earliest surviving Egyptian battlefield scene; kingship first imaged as total victory.
Scorpion Macehead
Predynastic · c. 3100 BCE
A limestone mace showing King Scorpion cutting a ceremonial canal. One of the earliest images of an Egyptian ruler — before the country was even unified.
Vessel
by Egyptian
Egypt · early dynastic (egyptian) · -3050–-2630 · Travertine (Egyptian alabaster)
Stele of King Djet (The Serpent King)
c. 3000 BCE · Limestone · Sully Wing
Over 5,000 years old — from the very dawn of writing. A serpent (the king's name) stands above the serekh, the palace façade, topped by Horus the falcon. This is what Egyptian writing looked like before it became hieroglyphs.
Bowl
by Egyptian
Egypt · old kingdom · -3000–-2613 · Travertine (Egyptian alabaster)
Heqat, the Frog Goddess
Predynastic · c. 2950 BCE
A small stone frog-headed goddess — Heqat, goddess of childbirth. Even at the dawn of Egypt, gods had animal forms and midwives invoked Heqat in labor.
Squat Dummy Jar with Lug Handles
Egypt, probably Saqqara, north of the Step Pyramid, excavations of J. E. Quibell, 1910-1911, Early Dynastic (2950–2647 BCE), Dynasties 2–3 · 2770–2573 BCE · sandstone
Shallow Dish
Egypt, Saqqara, Early Dynastic (2950–2647 BCE), Dynasty 2, reign of Nynetjer (c. 2700 BCE) or later · c. 2726–2647 BCE · travertine
Jar
by Egyptian
Egypt · old kingdom · -2707–-2219 · Stone
Eye of Horus (Wedjat) Amulet
by Egyptian
Egypt · old kingdom · -2686–-2125 · Carnelian
Pyramid of Djoser
Old Kingdom · c. 2670 BCE · Saqqara
The first pyramid in Egypt — six stacked mastabas designed by the architect Imhotep, later deified as a god of wisdom and medicine.
Tall Jar with Rope Patterns
Egypt, Saqqara, north of the Step Pyramid, tomb 2305, excavations of J. E. Quibell, 1910-1911, Old Kingdom (2647–2124 BCE), Dynasty 3, reign of Djoser (c. 2650 BCE)–reign of Sneferu (2613–2589 BCE) · 2647–2454 BCE · travertine
Pyramid of Meidum
Old Kingdom · c. 2600 BCE
A failed smooth-sided pyramid whose outer casing collapsed. What survives is the blunt tower at the core — an architectural experiment that taught the builders of Giza.
Bent Pyramid
Old Kingdom · Sneferu · c. 2600 BCE · Dahshur
Sneferu's second pyramid — the angle changes partway up, either to prevent collapse or because ambition had to meet physics. A crucial step between stepped and smooth pyramids.
Rahotep and Nofret
Old Kingdom · 4th Dynasty · c. 2600 BCE
Painted limestone seated pair from Meidum. Rock-crystal eyes so convincing that workmen at the excavation are said to have fled. Among the earliest masterpieces of Egyptian portraiture.
Meidum Geese
Old Kingdom · c. 2600 BCE · Meidum
A painted frieze of six geese from Nefermaat's mastaba chapel — species recognizably rendered, colors preserved like yesterday's paint. One of the freshest-feeling images from 4,600 years ago.
Red Pyramid
Old Kingdom · Sneferu · c. 2575 BCE · Dahshur
Sneferu's third pyramid — the first true smooth-sided pyramid to work. Named for the reddish limestone exposed after the original white casing was stripped for later buildings.
Spouted Bowl
Egypt, Old Kingdom (2647–2124 BCE), Dynasty 4 · c. 2573–2454 BCE · anorthosite gneiss
Khafre Enthroned
Old Kingdom · c. 2570 BCE · Giza
Diorite portrait of the pharaoh who built the second pyramid. Horus the falcon wraps his wings around the back of the king's head — divine kingship locked in stone.
Seated Statue of Hemiunu
Dynasty IV · c. 2570 BCE · Giza
A life-size limestone portrait of the vizier who is thought to have overseen construction of Khufu's Great Pyramid. His ample stomach and heavy jowls were a deliberate display of elite wealth — the opposite of the idealized kingly physique.
Great Pyramid of Giza
Old Kingdom · c. 2560 BCE
Tomb of Khufu. 2.3 million stone blocks, averaging 2.5 tons each. The tallest human-made structure on earth for 3,800 years.
The Seated Scribe
Old Kingdom · c. 2620–2500 BCE · Saqqara
Painted limestone, eyes inlaid with crystal and copper. Alert and ready, reed in hand — the realist portrait of a bureaucrat, 4,500 years ago.
Reserve Heads
Dynasty IV · c. 2550 BCE · Giza
Limestone heads placed in Old Kingdom tombs as substitute bodies in case the mummy was destroyed. Their ears are deliberately chipped off or their necks grooved — probably a ritual 'killing' to prevent the spirit from wandering back into the land of the living.
Ivory Statuette of Khufu
Old Kingdom · 4th Dynasty · c. 2550 BCE
Three inches tall — the only confirmed surviving image of the pharaoh who built the Great Pyramid. Found by Flinders Petrie at Abydos, headless, and discovered among the dirt on a sieve.
Stela of Tjenti and Nefret
by Egyptian; probably tomb G 3035, Giza, Egypt
Egypt · old kingdom · -2524–-2400 · Limestone
Tomb Wall Fragment Depicting Donkeys
by Egyptian
Egypt · old kingdom · -2504–-2347 · Limestone and pigment
Great Sphinx of Giza
Old Kingdom · c. 2500 BCE
Carved from a single limestone outcrop, the oldest known monumental sculpture. Its nose was already missing before Napoleon arrived.
Amulet of a Leg and Foot
by Egyptian
Egypt · old kingdom · -2494–-2055 · Carnelian
Triad of Menkaure
Old Kingdom · 4th Dynasty · c. 2490 BCE
Pharaoh Menkaure flanked by the goddess Hathor and a nome personification. One of a set from his Giza valley temple — divine companionship made into state sculpture.
Lintel of Enseperi
Egypt, Giza, western cemetery, excavations of Montague Ballard, 1901–2, Old Kingdom (2647–2124 BCE), Dynasty 5 · 2454–2311 BCE · limestone
Statue of Ranofer
Old Kingdom · 5th Dynasty · c. 2450 BCE
High Priest of Ptah at Memphis, carved standing in limestone with painted black wig and white kilt. One of the finest Old Kingdom private statues — an Egyptian bureaucrat granted the permanence of a god.
Head of Userkaf
Old Kingdom · 5th Dynasty · c. 2450 BCE
A greywacke head of the pharaoh who founded the 5th Dynasty and built the first sun temple. The crown is lost; the drilled pupils still look directly at you.
Headrest of Irut
Old Kingdom · c. 2450 BCE
A small alabaster headrest from a tomb — Egyptians slept with wooden or stone blocks under their necks in life and in death. Irut's name is inscribed in the base.
Tomb Wall Fragment Depicting Offering Bearers and Butchers
by Egyptian; Saqqara
Egypt · old kingdom · -2445–-2287 · Limestone
Seated Statue of Nykara
Egypt, Old Kingdom (2647–2124 BCE), Dynasty 5, reign of Nyuserre (2445–2422 BCE) or later · 2408–2377 BCE · red granite and pigment
Mastaba of Ti
Old Kingdom · c. 2400 BCE · Saqqara
The tomb of a minor royal official, painted inside with daily-life scenes — farming, fishing, ships under sail — and regarded as the high point of Old Kingdom relief.
Palermo Stone
Old Kingdom royal annals · c. 2400 BCE · Egypt
A black basalt slab inscribed with the reigns, heights of Nile floods, and major rituals of every king from the predynastic era through Dynasty V. Only seven fragments survive of what must have been a two-meter-tall master chronology.
Pyramid Texts
Old Kingdom · c. 2400 BCE · Saqqara
The oldest religious corpus on earth, carved into the walls of royal burial chambers at Saqqara. Written in Old Egyptian and painted turquoise, the spells teach the dead king how to fly to the sky, evade demons, and rejoin his father the sun.
Striding Statue of Minnefer
Old Kingdom · c. 2370 BCE
A painted limestone standing figure of a royal nobleman, left foot advanced in the canonical Egyptian stride. One of the most complete private statues from Giza's west field.
Pyramid of Unas
Dynasty V · c. 2345 BCE · Saqqara
A modest pyramid whose burial chamber is inscribed from floor to ceiling with the Pyramid Texts — the earliest religious writings known. Blue-painted hieroglyphs cover 283 spells guiding the king's ascent to the imperishable stars.
Relief of Agricultural Scenes
Egypt, Saqqara, Old Kingdom (2647–2124 BCE), Dynasty 6 · c. 2311–2281 BCE · painted limestone
Inscribed Relief
Egypt, Saqqara, Old Kingdom (2647–2124 BCE), Dynasty 6 · c. 2311–2281 BCE · painted limestone
Relief of Three Offering Bearers
Egypt, Saqqara, Old Kingdom (2647–2124 BCE), Dynasty 6 · c. 2311–2281 BCE · painted limestone
Relief of Men Bringing Desert Animals
Egypt, Saqqara, Old Kingdom (2647–2124 BCE), Dynasty 6 · c. 2311–2281 BCE · limestone
Relief of Men Bringing Birds and Cattle
Egypt, Saqqara, Old Kingdom (2647–2124 BCE), Dynasty 6 · c. 2311–2281 BCE · painted limestone
Saqqara Relief of Nyankhnesut
Old Kingdom · Dynasty 6 · c. 2300 BCE · painted limestone
A tomb relief of the 6th Dynasty official Nyankhnesut seated at an offering table — the convention locked in the Old Kingdom, looking forward to three millennia of imitation.
Great Sphinx of Tanis
Old to Middle Kingdom · c. 2600–1800 BCE
One of the largest sphinxes outside Giza — reused and re-inscribed by pharaoh after pharaoh. Now guards the entrance of the Louvre's Egyptian galleries.
Relief Fragment Depicting Ipi and Fowling in the Marshes
by Egyptian; Saqqara, Teti Pyramid Cemetery, offering niche of Ipi
Saqqara · first intermediate period · -2186–-2061 · Limestone with traces of pigment
Wall Fragment from a Tomb Depicting Offering Bearers
by Egyptian; Saqqara, tomb of Hetepmeret
Egypt · first intermediate period · -2186–-2061 · Limestone and pigment
Stele of Itetioqer and Family
Egypt, Southern Upper Egypt, Middle Kingdom (2040–1648 BCE), Dynasty 11 · c. 2123–2040 BCE · painted limestone
Relief Fragment Depicting Merettetiiyet with Offerings
by Egyptian; offering niche of Merettetiiyet, Teti Pyramid Cemetery, Saqqara, Egypt
Saqqara · first intermediate period · -2060–-2025 · Limestone and pigment
Wall Fragment from a Tomb Depicting a Harvest Scene
by Egyptian, Saqqara ?
Egypt · tenth dynasty · -2060–-2025 · Limestone
Wall Fragment from a Tomb Depicting a Herdsman
by Egyptian; Saqqara
Egypt · first intermediate period · -2060–-2025 · Limestone
Figure
Egypt · c. 2050 BC - c. 1650 BC
Model of a River Boat
by Egyptian; Meir, Egypt
Egypt · middle kingdom · -2046–-1794 · Wood and pigment
Wooden Staff or Support
Egypt, Middle Kingdom (2040–1648 BCE), Dynasties 11–12 · 2030–1650 BCE · wood
Statue of Mentuhotep II
Dynasty XI · c. 2030 BCE · Deir el-Bahari
The unifier of Egypt after the First Intermediate Period, painted with the black skin of Osiris and wearing the red crown of Lower Egypt. His deliberately massive, almost archaic body announces a new Middle Kingdom style grounded in reborn Upper Egyptian traditions.
Karnak Temple Complex
New Kingdom onward · 2000 BCE – 100 CE
A city of temples at Thebes, built and rebuilt by thirty dynasties. The hypostyle hall alone has 134 columns.
Kerma
Kerma culture · c. 2500–1500 BCE · Sudan
Capital of the first kingdom of Nubia, whose massive mud-brick Western Deffufa still rises 18 meters. The royal tumuli concealed astonishing human sacrifices — hundreds of courtiers buried alive around their king to serve him in the afterlife.
Head of Mentuhotep III
Middle Kingdom · c. 2000 BCE
A painted sandstone head, eyes rimmed with cosmetic black — possibly the shadowy eleventh-dynasty pharaoh whose reign pivoted Egypt from Thebes back to a unified state.
Ring with a Scarab Bezel
by Egyptian
Egypt · middle kingdom · -1985–-1550 · Gold and green jasper
Kohl Jar with Lid
by Egyptian
Egypt · middle kingdom · -1985–-1773 · Travertine (Egyptian alabaster)
Ointment Jar with Lid
by Egyptian
Egypt · twelfth dynasty · -1985–-1773 · Anhydrite
Mortar and Pestle
by Egyptian
Egypt · twelfth dynasty · -1985–-1773 · Mortar: baked steatite; pestle: granite
Canopic Jar
by Egyptian
Egypt · middle kingdom · -1985–-1773 · Travertine (Egyptian alabaster)
Models from the Tomb of Meketre
Dynasty XI · c. 1981 BCE · Thebes
Dozens of painted wooden miniatures — a cattle count, weaving workshops, porticoed villas, fishing boats — sealed in a hidden chamber beside Meketre's tomb. Discovered intact in 1920, they provide the most detailed snapshot of Middle Kingdom daily life.
Cosmetic Vessel
Egypt, Middle Kingdom (2040–1648 BCE), Dynasty 12 · c. 1980–1901 BCE · anhydrite
Sidelocks and Elements of Wig Decoration
Egypt, Middle Kingdom (2040–1648 BCE), Dynasty 12 · 1980–1801 BCE · Electrum (?) over a calcite gesso; silver
Cosmetic Vessel (Cylinder Beaker)
Egypt, Middle Kingdom (2040–1648 BCE), Dynasty 12 · 1980–1801 BCE · travertine
Sidelock (one of a pair)
Egypt, Middle Kingdom (2040–1648 BCE), Dynasty 12 · 1980–1801 BCE · Electrum (?) over a calcite gesso
Rosette for Wig Decoration
Egypt, Middle Kingdom (2040–1648 BCE), Dynasty 12 · 1980–1801 BCE · silver
Element of Wig Decoration
Egypt, Middle Kingdom (2040–1648 BCE), Dynasty 12 · 1980–1801 BCE · silver
Plaque
Egypt · c. 1971 BC - c. 1926 BC
Stele of Shemai
Egypt, Aswan, Qubbet el-Hawa, excavations of Lady William Cecil, 1904, "Cecil Tombs," no. 28, Middle Kingdom (2040–1648 BCE), Dynasty 12, probably reign of Senusret I (1971–1926 BCE) · c. 1960–1916 BCE · painted limestone
Stela of Amenemhat and Hemet
by Egyptian; probably Thebes
Egypt · twelfth dynasty · -1956–-1877 · Limestone and pigment
White Chapel of Senusret I
Middle Kingdom · c. 1950 BCE · Karnak
A small bark chapel of fine white limestone. Torn down as filler in a later pylon; found reassembled piece by piece and reconstructed in the 1930s.
Prisse Papyrus
Middle Kingdom · c. 1900 BCE
The oldest literary book in existence. Two teachings of wisdom — "the Instructions of Kagemni" and "of Ptahhotep" — carefully written in cursive hieroglyphs.
Beni Hasan
Middle Kingdom · c. 1900 BCE
Thirty-nine rock-cut tombs of provincial governors, walls painted with daily life — wrestling matches, bakers, looms, dancers — a rare survival of Middle Kingdom domestic art.
William the Hippopotamus
Middle Kingdom · c. 1961–1878 BCE
A small blue-green faience hippo, legs deliberately broken before burial to keep it from attacking the dead in the afterlife. Painted with lotuses; the unofficial mascot of the Met.
Coffin of Senbi
Middle Kingdom · c. 1900 BCE · Meir
A painted wooden coffin lid from Meir, carved and painted with a detailed facade — windows, doors, decorative patterns. A Middle Kingdom coffin was a miniature house for the afterlife.
Stela of Amenemhat and Yatu
by Egyptian; possibly Memphis, Egypt
Egypt · middle kingdom · -1870–-1770 · Limestone and pigment
Cosmetic Vessel (Beaker)
Egypt, Middle Kingdom (2040–1648 BCE), Dynasty 12, reign of Amenemhat III (1860–1814 BCE) · c. 1859–1814 BCE · obsidian and gold
Head of Senusret III
Middle Kingdom · c. 1850 BCE
A careworn face cut from hard stone — heavy eyelids, downturned mouth. The first royal portraits that reads as an aging man rather than a god, and the Middle Kingdom's break with Old Kingdom idealism.
Statue of Amenemhat III
Middle Kingdom · c. 1850 BCE
A granite statue of the 12th Dynasty pharaoh in the brooding Middle Kingdom portrait style — heavy eyelids, set jaw. He built the twin pyramids at Dahshur and reclaimed the Fayum oasis.
Middle Kingdom Statuette of a Man
Middle Kingdom · c. 1850 BCE
A small striding wooden figure of a scribe or official — smaller, more private statues proliferated in the Middle Kingdom, placed in modest tombs beside big official statues.
Head of an Official
by Egyptian
Egypt · middle kingdom · -1773–-1650 · Granite
Mirror
Egypt · 1587 BC - 1328 BC
Seated Queen (Second Intermediate)
Second Intermediate · c. 1580 BCE
The upper half of a black granite queen, broken at the waist. Neither her name nor the king she sat beside survives; her features are finely Kushite, from a murky century when Egypt's south broke away.
Shabti (Funerary Figurine) of Nebseni
by Egyptian; Thebes
Egypt · new kingdom · -1570–-1292 · Wood (tamarisk) and pigment
Furnishing fabric
Thebes · 1570 BC-1293 BC
Book of the Dead
New Kingdom onward · 1550 BCE – 50 BCE
Spells written on papyrus to guide the deceased through the Duat. The Weighing of the Heart against a feather is its most famous scene.
Beads
Egypt · c. 1550 BC - c. 1077 BC
Hand Mirror
by Egyptian
Egypt · new kingdom · -1550–-1295 · Copper alloy
Cosmetic Saucer Decorated with Swans
by Egyptian
Egypt · new kingdom · -1550–-1070 · Stone
Amulet of a Heart
by Egyptian
Egypt · eighteenth dynasty · -1550–-1295 · Glass
Bead
Egypt · c. 1550 BC - c. 1077 BC
Fitting
Egypt · c. 1550 BC - c. 1077 BC
Ointment Vessel with Lid
by Egyptian
Egypt · egyptian · -1550–-1295 · Serpentinite
Ointment Vessel
by Egyptian
Egypt · new kingdom · -1550–-1295 · Travertine (Egyptian alabaster)
Toilet tray
Egypt · c. 1550 BC - c. 1077 BC
Ear ornament
Egypt · 1550 BC-1295 BC
Glass fragment
Egypt · 1550 BC-1295 BC
Head
Egypt · 1550 BC - 1292 BC
Unguent Bottle (Amphoriskos)
Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasties 18–19 · 1549–1296 BCE · glass
Giraffe Head
Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18 · c. 1540–1296 BCE · ivory
Vessel in the Form of an Ostrich Egg
Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18 · c. 1540–1296 BCE · travertine
Canopic Jar with Lid
Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18 · 1540–1296 BCE · limestone
Bottle
by Egyptian
Egypt · eighteenth dynasty · -1505–-1202 · Glass
Caryatid Mirror
New Kingdom · c. 1500 BCE
A bronze mirror whose handle is a cast nude woman supporting the polished disk on her head. Mirrors were gifts for women's tombs, often with handle-figures of beauty goddesses.
Heart Scarab of Nefer
New Kingdom · Dynasty 18 · c. 1500 BCE · graywacke
A greywacke heart scarab, placed over the mummy's chest, with a Book of the Dead spell on its underside asking the owner's heart not to testify against him at judgment.
Seated Scribe of Medthu
Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Tuthmosis III (1479–1425 BCE) · c. 1479–1425 BCE · limestone, originally painted
Pomegranate Vase
Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Tuthmosis III (1479–1425 BCE) or later · 1479–1425 BCE · blue vitreous faience
Seated Pair Statue
Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Tuthmosis III (1479–1425 BCE) · c. 1479–1425 BCE · painted limestone
Domestic Shrine
Egypt, Thebes(?), New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Tuthmosis III (1479–1425 BCE) · c. 1479–1425 BCE · limestone, originally painted
Temple of Hatshepsut
New Kingdom · c. 1470 BCE · Deir el-Bahari
Three stacked colonnaded terraces built into a cliff for Egypt's longest-reigning female pharaoh. Her successor tried to erase her — and failed.
Hatshepsut Cowroid Seal
New Kingdom · c. 1470 BCE
A tiny steatite seal-amulet inscribed for "God's Wife of Amun, Hatshepsut" — before she crowned herself pharaoh. Held like a signet, pressed into clay to mark her possessions.
Kneeling Statue of Hatshepsut
New Kingdom · 18th Dynasty · c. 1470 BCE
A large granite statue of Hatshepsut kneeling to offer libation jars — dressed as king, with male royal regalia, beard attached. The female pharaoh as she wished to be remembered.
Hatshepsut Ointment Jar
New Kingdom · c. 1470 BCE
A translucent travertine jar inscribed for Hatshepsut — ointment jars like this accompanied royal tombs in the hundreds. The jar still holds residue of the original scented fat.
Temple of Amun at Jebel Barkal
Kushite · c. 1450 BCE onward · Sudan
Built by Thutmose III below a sandstone butte the Nubians believed Amun lived inside. Reused by Kushite pharaohs who conquered Egypt from here in the 25th Dynasty.
Gebel Barkal
Kushite sacred mountain · c. 1450 BCE onward · Sudan
A 98-meter sandstone butte the Kushites believed housed the creator-god Amun. The New Kingdom pharaohs built a temple at its base and the Napatan kings inscribed a pinnacle-shaped outcrop with gilded reliefs now almost vanished to weather.
Tomb of Rekhmire
New Kingdom · 18th Dynasty · c. 1450 BCE
Vizier of Thutmose III, buried in Sheikh Abd el-Qurna. The tomb walls are a painted bureaucracy — foreign tribute, brickmaking, granary counts. One of the fullest social portraits of the New Kingdom.
Paint Box of Vizier Amenemope
Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Amenhotep II (1425–1400 BCE) · c. 1427–1401 BCE · boxwood with inscription inlaid in Egyptian blue
Canopic Jar of Amenhotep
by Egyptian; Tomb A7, Dra Abu el-Naga, Thebes
Egypt · new kingdom · -1427–-1400 · Ceramic and pigment
Sceptre
Egypt · c. 1427 BC - c. 1401 BC
Fragment of a Tomb Relief
Egypt, probably Thebes, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Tuthmosis II (1493–1479 BCE) or later · 1401–1391 BCE · limestone
Male Head, perhaps from a Pair Statue
Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Amenhotep II (1425–1400 BCE)–reign of Tuthmosis IV (1400–1390 BCE) · 1401–1391 BCE · gray granite
Deir el-Medina
New Kingdom · c. 1550–1080 BCE
The workers' village for those who built the royal tombs. Its own tombs are painted with a precision surprising for common craftsmen — because the craftsmen were the painters of the Valley of the Kings.
Tomb of Nakht
New Kingdom · 18th Dynasty · c. 1400 BCE
A small painted tomb for a granary scribe and astronomer. The banquet scene — three women musicians, a blind harpist, guests at table — is one of the most reproduced images from Thebes.
Earring
by Egyptian
Egypt · new kingdom · -1400–-1295 · Glass
Nome Gods Bearing Offerings
Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Amenhotep III (1390–1352 BCE) · c. 1391–1353 BCE · painted limestone
Statuette of a Man
Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Amenhotep III (1390–1352 BCE) · c. 1391–1353 BCE · ebony inlaid with cobalt blue glass
Hedgehog
Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Amenhotep III (1390–1352 BCE) · c. 1391–1353 BCE · steatite, originally glazed
Head of Amenhotep III Wearing the Round Wig
Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Amenhotep III (1390–1352 BCE) · c. 1391–1353 BCE · brown quartzite
Head of Sekhmet
Egypt, Thebes(?), New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Amenhotep III (1390–1352 BCE) · c. 1391–1353 BCE · granodiorite
Ring
Egypt · c. 1391 BC - c. 1077 BC
Face from a Cosmetic Spoon
Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Amenhotep III (1390–1352 BCE) · 1391–1353 BCE · ivory
Comb with Gazelle
Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Amenhotep III (1390–1352 BCE) · c. 1391–1353 BCE · wood
Petal-Shaped Bead
Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Amenhotep III (1390–1352 BCE) · c. 1391–1353 BCE · faience
Lotus Blossom Necklace Terminal
Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Amenhotep III (1390–1352 BCE)–reign of Akhenaten (1351–1334 BCE) · c. 1391–1337 BCE · polychrome faience with inlaid and painted decoration
Amenhotep III in the Blue Crown
New Kingdom · c. 1390 BCE
A granite head of Amenhotep III wearing the khepresh — the leather-studded battle-crown usually worn for war. The father of Akhenaten rules at Egypt's peak.
Avenue of Sphinxes
New Kingdom–Ptolemaic · c. 1380 BCE onward · Luxor
A 2.7-kilometer processional road flanked by more than 1,300 ram-headed and human-headed sphinxes connecting Luxor Temple to Karnak. Reopened in 2021 after twenty years of excavation stripped away centuries of silt and medieval housing.
Frog Amulet
Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18 · c. 1380–1330 BCE · polychrome faience
Fragment of an Inlay Headdress
Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18 · c. 1353–1295 BCE · lavender blue faience with deep blue overglaze
Head From a Shabti (Funerary Figurine) of Queen Tiye
Egypt · new kingdom · -1353–-1336 · Travertine (Egyptian alabaster) with traces of pigment
Finger Ring with Frog
Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Akhenaten (1351–1334 BCE) · c. 1353–1337 BCE · gold
Talatat: Portrait of Nefertiti
Egypt, Karnak, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Akhenaten (1351–1334 BCE) · c. 1353–1347 BCE · painted sandstone
Talatat: Nefertiti Offers to the Aten
Egypt, Karnak, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Akhenaten (1351–1334 BCE) · 1353–1347 BCE · painted sandstone
Fragment of a Cosmetic Dish
Egypt, Amarna, North Suburb, house T.34.1. Excavations of the Egypt Exploration Society, 1930, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Akhenaten (1351–1334 BCE) · 1353–1337 BCE · travertine
Blue-Painted Jar
Egypt, Amarna, house T.36.54. Excavations of the Egypt Exploration Society, 1930, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Akhenaten (1351–1334 BCE) · 1353–1337 BCE · Nile silt ware
Manufacturing waste
Egypt · 1353 BC-1335 BC
Kohl Container in the Shape of a Palm Column
by Egyptian
Egypt · eighteenth dynasty · -1352–-1213 · Glass
Colossi of Memnon
New Kingdom · c. 1350 BCE · Luxor
Twin seated statues of Amenhotep III, sixty feet tall. After an earthquake cracked one, it "sang" at dawn for two centuries — until Romans repaired it.
Colossal Statue of Akhenaten
Amarna period · c. 1350 BCE
The heretic pharaoh depicted with a long face, wide hips, and swollen belly — a deliberate break from centuries of idealized royal portraiture.
Amarna Letters
New Kingdom · 14th c. BCE
Clay tablets of diplomatic correspondence between Egypt and its neighbors — Babylonia, Assyria, the Hittites, Canaanite city-states. Written in Akkadian, the lingua franca of the Bronze Age.
Tomb-Chapel of Nebamun
Dynasty XVIII · c. 1350 BCE · Thebes
Painted fragments from a scribe's vanished tomb, including the famous scene of Nebamun fowling in a marsh with a spotted cat retrieving birds. The butterflies in flight are rendered with a freedom the Egyptian grid rarely allowed.
Talatat of Nefertiti
Amarna · c. 1350 BCE · Karnak
A small sandstone block from Akhenaten's dismantled Karnak temple — a tightly cropped profile of Nefertiti, already in the exaggerated Amarna style. 45,000 talatat blocks have been recovered.
Bust of Nefertiti
Amarna period · c. 1345 BCE
Painted limestone, found in the workshop of the sculptor Thutmose at Amarna. Still in Berlin; Egypt has asked for it back for a century.
Amarna Princess
Amarna · 18th Dynasty · c. 1345 BCE
A small red quartzite head of a young princess from Akhenaten's new capital. The elongated skull and naturalistic features are pure Amarna — the brief revolution in Egyptian form that died with its king.
Purification of Queen Kiya
Amarna · c. 1345 BCE
A painted limestone relief fragment — attendants pour libation over a royal woman, probably Kiya, Akhenaten's secondary queen. Her name was later chiseled out in an Amarna damnatio memoriae.
Lintel Fragment Depicting Iniuia and Iuy Worshipping Deities
by Egyptian; Tomb of Iniuia and Iuy, Saqqara, Egypt
Egypt · new kingdom · -1336–-1327 · Limestone and pigment
Unguent Bottle (Krateriskos)
Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, post-Amarna period or later · c. 1336–1295 BCE · glass
Amulet of Taweret
by Egyptian; Probably Tell el-Amarna, Egypt
Egypt · new kingdom · -1336 · Glass
Lentoid Flask (Pilgrim Flask)
Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasties 18–19 · c. 1336–1186 BCE · glass
Luxor Temple
New Kingdom · Amenhotep III / Ramesses II · c. 1400–1250 BCE
Every year during the Opet Festival, statues of the gods were carried in boats from Karnak to here. A Roman camp, Christian church, and mosque all followed.
Mask of Tutankhamun
New Kingdom · c. 1323 BCE
Eleven kilograms of solid gold, inlaid with lapis, carnelian, and obsidian. The funerary mask of a boy-king who died at nineteen.
Tomb of Tutankhamun
18th Dynasty · c. 1323 BCE · Valley of the Kings
A small, hastily decorated tomb, overlooked by thieves for three thousand years. Howard Carter opened it in 1922 and the world went Egyptomaniac again.
Tomb of Tutankhamun Interior (KV62)
Dynasty XVIII · c. 1323 BCE · Valley of the Kings
Yellow walls densely painted with the funerary Opening of the Mouth rite and Tutankhamun being greeted by Osiris. Oddly cramped for a king, the tomb was almost certainly borrowed after his unexpected death at about nineteen.
Statuette of a Serving Girl
Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasties 18–19 · c. 1323–1186 BCE · terracotta, originally painted
Uraeus (Rearing Cobra)
Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 19 · 1307–1196 BCE · Silver-copper alloy with inlays (copper, feldspar, turquoise, obsidian?) and gilding
Valley of the Kings
New Kingdom · c. 1550–1070 BCE · West Bank, Thebes
Sixty-five painted tombs cut into the limestone valley. The pharaohs chose it for its isolation — looters found everything anyway, except Tutankhamun.
Serapeum of Saqqara
New Kingdom to Ptolemaic · c. 1300 BCE onward · Saqqara
Underground vaults housing the mummies of the sacred Apis bulls, each sealed in a granite sarcophagus weighing up to seventy tonnes. How the Egyptians maneuvered them down the narrow subterranean corridors is still debated.
Shabti (Funerary Figurine) of Mayat
by Egyptian; probably Thebes
Egypt · new kingdom · -1295–-1186 · Wood
Ostracon with a Drawing of a King
by Egyptian
Egypt · new kingdom · -1295–-1069 · Limestone and pigment
Reliefs from the Tomb of Seti I
c. 1290 BCE · Painted limestone · Sully Wing
The goddess Hathor welcomes Seti I into the afterlife. Cut from the walls of KV17 in the Valley of the Kings in the 19th century — a practice we now consider vandalism, but which saved this relief from the flooding that ravaged the tomb.
Temple of Seti I at Abydos
New Kingdom · c. 1290 BCE
An L-shaped temple with seven chapels. Its Abydos King List carved on one corridor became a key scholarly tool — naming 76 pharaohs back to the First Dynasty.
Tomb of Sennedjem
New Kingdom · 19th Dynasty · c. 1290 BCE
A painted tomb at Deir el-Medina for a craftsman of the royal necropolis — its vaulted ceiling covered in the Field of Reeds, Osiris, and the afterworld's ploughing harvests.
Great Hypostyle Hall of Karnak
Dynasty XIX · c. 1280 BCE · Karnak
A forest of 134 lotus-bud and papyrus-bloom columns covering half a hectare, the tallest reaching 21 meters. Seti I and Ramesses II finished its decoration; inside, the sandstone sky was painted a dense blue and dotted with yellow stars.
Tomb of Seti I
19th Dynasty · c. 1279 BCE · Valley of the Kings
The longest, deepest, and most richly painted tomb in the valley. Every wall is covered in funerary texts; ceilings map the stars.
Ostracon: Ramesses II Suckled by a Goddess
Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 19, reign of Ramesses II (1279–1213 BCE) · c. 1279–1213 BCE · painted limestone
Tomb Relief of the Chief Physician Amenhotep and Family
Egypt, Asyut, New Kingdom, (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 19, reign of Ramesses II (1279–1213 BCE) · c. 1279–1257 BCE · limestone with traces of paint
Abu Simbel
Reign of Ramesses II · c. 1264 BCE
Four colossal statues guard the temple front. In the 1960s the whole complex was cut apart and moved 200 feet up to escape Lake Nasser.
Tomb of Nefertari
19th dynasty · c. 1255 BCE · Valley of the Queens
Painted for Ramesses II's favorite wife — the queen walks through the underworld, plays the board game Senet, and greets the gods. Colors still vivid.
Ramesseum
New Kingdom · Ramesses II · 13th c. BCE · Thebes
Memorial temple of Ramesses II. A collapsed colossus here inspired Shelley's "Ozymandias" — though Shelley never saw it.
Younger Memnon (Ramesses II)
New Kingdom · c. 1250 BCE
A granite colossus of Ramesses II. Belzoni dragged it out of the Ramesseum in 1816 and shipped it to the British Museum. Shelley saw it — and wrote Ozymandias.
Colossal Statue of Ramesses II
New Kingdom · c. 1250 BCE · Grand Egyptian Museum
Eleven meters of carved granite, 83 tons. Moved from downtown Cairo to the Grand Egyptian Museum in a 2006 road spectacle so large they had to raise bridges.
Lion Subduing a Nubian
New Kingdom · Ramesside · c. 1250 BCE
A cosmetic-spoon handle of glazed blue and gold — a lion (Egypt) pinning a tied Nubian captive. Rhetoric of imperial power shrunk down to toiletry.
Fragment of a Stela of Neferhotep
by Egyptian; probably Theban Tomb 216, Deir el-Medina, Egypt
Egypt · new kingdom · -1239–-1194 · Limestone and pigment
Merneptah Stele
New Kingdom · 1208 BCE
Black granite stele listing the pharaoh's victories. Line 27 contains the earliest known mention of "Israel" in any historical record.
Tile
Egypt · c.1200 BC - c.1194 BC
Shabti of the Singer of Amun Inhai
by Egyptian
Egypt · new kingdom · -1186–-1069 · Ceramic and pigment
Medinet Habu
New Kingdom · Ramesses III · c. 1150 BCE · Thebes
The fortress-temple of the last great warrior pharaoh. Its walls narrate the repulse of the Sea Peoples — history preserved because the victor carved it.
Amulet of the Goddess Nephthys
by Egyptian

Funerary Mask of Psusennes I
Third Intermediate · 21st Dynasty · c. 990 BCE
Gold burial mask of a pharaoh found intact at Tanis in 1940 — one of the only royal Egyptian tombs ever found unlooted. The find was buried in war headlines and nearly forgotten until much later.
Shawabty of Pinudjem I, High Priest of Amen and King
Egypt, Third Intermediate (1069–715 BCE), Dynasty 21 · c. 990–969 BCE · bright blue faience with purple details
Amulet of a Ram-Headed Deity

Coffin and Mummy of Paankhaenamun
by Egyptian; Thebes
Thebes · twenty-second dynasty · -924–-889 · Cartonnage
Canopic Jars
New Kingdom onward · c. 1500 BCE – 1000 BCE
Four jars holding the mummy's liver, lungs, stomach, and intestines. Their lids shaped as the four sons of Horus — jackal, baboon, falcon, and human — each guarding a separate organ.
Amulet of the God Imsety (one of the four Sons of Horus)
by Egyptian
Egypt · late period (egyptian) · -780–-343 · Glass
Pyramids of El-Kurru
Napatan Kush · c. 860–600 BCE · Sudan
The earliest royal cemetery of the Kushite 25th Dynasty, where the pharaohs who briefly ruled Egypt lie beneath small, steep-sided pyramids. Inside, painted chambers fuse Egyptian afterlife iconography with distinctly African imagery of hunting and cattle.
Pendant
Sudan, Napatan; lion's head component: probably Egypt, Third Intermediate (1069–715 BCE), Dynasty 25 · c. 700 BCE · amethyst and gold
Amulet of the Goddess Isis Nursing the God Horus
by Egyptian
Egypt · third intermediate period · -747–-656 · Hematite
Victory Stele of Piye
Kushite · c. 727 BCE
A granite stele recording the Nubian king Piye's conquest of Egypt, written in formal Egyptian. His battle narrative is unusually vivid — detailed reprimands to his generals, pardons for vanquished princes.
Shawabty of Ditamenpaankh
Egypt, Late period (715–332 BCE), Dynasty 25 · c. 715–656 BCE · faience
Shawabty Box of Ditamenpaankh

Covered Box for Shawabtys of Ditamenpaankh
Egypt, Late period (715–332 BCE), Dynasty 25 · 715–656 BCE · painted wood
Shabaka Stone
Kushite (25th Dynasty) · c. 700 BCE
A Nubian pharaoh ordered an ancient Memphite theology copied onto this granite slab because the papyrus original was crumbling. Centuries later, someone used the stone as a millstone, grinding away most of the text.

Sphinx of Taharqa
Nubian Kush · 25th Dynasty · c. 680 BCE
A Nubian pharaoh in the form of an Egyptian sphinx, carrying a double crown he had earned as conqueror. Now in the British Museum.

Pyramids of Nuri
Napatan Kush · c. 650–300 BCE · Sudan
The burial field of Taharqa, last of the Kushite Black Pharaohs, and twenty of his successors. Recent underwater excavations have plunged archaeologists into flooded burial chambers still partially filled with gilded coffin ornaments and shawabtis.
Gayer-Anderson Cat
Late Period · c. 600 BCE
A hollow-cast bronze cat in the form of the goddess Bastet, with gold earrings and nose ring. Slightly battered but uncannily alive — one of the most reproduced pieces in the British Museum.

Temple of Hibis
Late Period · 6th c. BCE · Kharga Oasis
The largest surviving temple of the Persian period in Egypt. Carved in sandstone in the middle of a desert oasis; its roof blocks depict 700 Egyptian deities.

Pyramids of Meroë
Kingdom of Kush · 300 BCE – 300 CE · Sudan
Over 200 pyramids, steeper and smaller than Egypt's, built for Nubian royalty. Sudan has more pyramids than Egypt — about twice as many.
Apis Bull Statue
Ptolemaic · 4th–2nd c. BCE
A striding stone Apis bull — the sacred bull of Memphis, worshipped as living god and mummified when it died. Greco-Egyptian in style, made when Egypt's rulers spoke Greek and still honored the ancient cults.
Sandal
Egypt · ca. 30 BC - AD 300
Trial Piece with Hieroglyphs

by Greek, Ptolemaic; minted in Egypt
Alexandria · ptolemaic · -221–-205 · Gold
Octodrachm: Head of Arsinoe II (obverse); Double Cornucopia (reverse)
Greek, Ptolemaic, minted at Alexandreia (Egypt), reign of Ptolemy V Epiphanes or Ptolemy VI Philometor · 205–145 BCE · gold
Musawwarat es-Sufra
Meroitic · 3rd c. BCE onward · Sudan
A sprawling Meroitic temple complex in open desert — elephants carved on column bases, ramps wide enough for live ones to climb. A Kushite religious center whose original rituals are still largely mysterious.
Taweret Statuette
Ptolemaic · 332–30 BCE
A blue faience figure of Taweret, the hippo-headed goddess who protected mothers and newborns. She stands on her hind legs, swollen-bellied, holding the protective sa-sign.
Mit Rahina Ware Vessel
Egypt, Greco-Roman period (332 BCE–395 CE), Ptolemaic dynasty (305–30 BCE) · 200–100 BCE · polychrome faience
Torso of Amenpayom
Egypt, Greco-Roman period (332 BCE–395 CE), Ptolemaic dynasty (305–30 BCE) · probably 200–100 BCE · granodiorite
Askos
Greece, Ptolemaic Dynasty · 200–1 BCE · agate and gold
Rosetta Stone
Ptolemaic · 196 BCE
A decree written three times — in hieroglyphs, Demotic, and Greek — that became the key to reading Egyptian. Champollion cracked it in 1822.
Kom Ombo
Ptolemaic · 2nd c. BCE
A rare double temple — one half for the crocodile god Sobek, the other for Horus the Elder. Mummified crocodiles still fill a side chamber.
Coin Depicting the God Zeus
by Greek, Ptolemaic; minted in Egypt
Egypt · ptolemaic · -117–-111 · Copper
Vulture Headdress Inlay
Ptolemaic · c. 100 BCE
A tiny gold-glass inlay of a vulture headdress — a fragment from a larger votive mask. Vulture headdresses crowned goddesses like Mut and Nekhbet.
Book of the Dead of Buiruhar(mut)
Egypt, Third Intermediate (1069–715 BCE), Dynasties 21–22 · 1000–900 BCE · papyrus
Funerary Mask
by Egyptian; probably from Hawara, Egypt
Egypt · egyptian · -100–-1 · Cartonnage
Linen shroud
Egypt · 100-101 BC
Amulet of Isis
Egypt, Greco-Roman period (332 BCE–395 CE), Roman empire (30 BCE–395 CE) · 100 BCE–100 CE · deep blue glass
Tetradrachm (Coin) Portraying King Ptolemy of Cyprus
by Greek, Ptolemaic
Cyprus · ptolemaic · -65–-64 · Silver
Dendera Temple
Ptolemaic → Roman · 54 BCE – 20 CE
Temple of Hathor. Its astronomical ceilings include the only known circular zodiac from ancient Egypt — now a replica; the original is in the Louvre.
Dendera Zodiac
Ptolemaic · c. 50 BCE
A circular sandstone relief of the night sky carried off from the ceiling of Dendera temple by a French explorer in 1821. Now in the Louvre; a replica hangs in the original.
Cartonnage Mummy Case
Egypt, Greco-Roman period (332 BCE–395 CE), Ptolemaic dynasty (305–30 BCE)–Roman empire (30 BCE–395 CE) · c. 50 BCE–50 CE · cartonnage, painted and gilded, with glass inlays
Temple of Dendur
Roman Egypt · 15 BCE
A small sandstone temple given to the United States in 1965 when Lake Nasser was about to drown it. Reassembled stone by stone inside the Met.

Funerary Portrait of a Young Girl
Egypt, Roman Empire, late Tiberian · c. 25–37 CE · encaustic on linden wood
Apedemak Temple at Naqa
Meroitic · 1st c. CE · Sudan
A Kushite temple to the lion-god Apedemak. The reliefs show the king and queen in a distinctly African body type, inscriptions in Meroitic — a script still not fully deciphered.
Necklace with Pendant
by Roman; probably Egypt
Roman Empire · roman period (egyptian) · 101–300 · Gold
Portrait of a Man Wearing an Ivy Wreath
by Egyptian; The Fayum, Egypt
Al Fayyum · roman period (egyptian) · 101–150 · Lime (linden) wood

by Egyptian; The Fayum, Egypt
Al Fayyum · roman period (egyptian) · 101–150 · Lime (linden) wood
Funerary Portrait of a Woman
Egypt, Roman Empire, Antonine · c. 138–92 CE · encaustic on linen
Fayum Mummy Portraits
Roman Egypt · 1st–3rd c. CE
Encaustic panels placed over the faces of mummies. The most personal, most realistic portraits to survive from the ancient world — they still look you in the eye.
Catacombs of Kom el Shoqafa
Roman Egypt · 2nd c. CE · Alexandria
Three levels of rock-cut tombs where Egyptian gods, Roman legionaries, and Hellenistic motifs stand on the same walls. Alexandria's last great pagan monument, discovered in 1900 when a donkey fell through the roof.
Collar with Medallion and Pendant
Egypt, Alexandria, Roman · Collar: c. 200–300 CE, Coin: c. 177 CE · gold
Fragment of a Necklace
by Roman; probably Egypt
Roman Empire · greco-roman (egyptian) · 201–300 · Gold
Temple of Edfu
Ptolemaic · 237–57 BCE
The best-preserved temple in Egypt, because it was buried in desert sand for centuries. Dedicated to Horus, with reliefs of his battle against Seth.
Fragment with Satyr and Maenad
Byzantine Empire (Egypt) · 300s CE · Linen: undyed; wool: dyed; plain weave ground with tapestry weave

by Byzantine; Egypt
Ancient Egypt · coptic (period) · 401–600 · Wood

500 – 1000

Coptic Curtain Panel with Merrymaking
Coptic Egypt · 6th c. · linen and wool tapestry
A tapestry-woven panel with dancers and drinkers among vines — Coptic Egypt decorated private houses with pagan-style merrymaking long after the Theodosian edicts had closed the temples.
Coptic Virgin and Child Tapestry
Coptic Egypt · 6th c. · wool tapestry
A wool tapestry panel of the Virgin enthroned with Christ — one of the earliest surviving Virgin-and-Child images, woven at roughly the moment Byzantine painters were fixing the icon format at Sinai.
Resist-Dyed Hanging with Biblical Scenes
Egypt, Byzantine period · 500–550 · linen: resist-dyed, plain weave
Tunic Fragment with Segmentum

by Egypt
Egypt · coptic (historically identified as) · 501–700 · Linen and wool
Coptic Icon of Christ and Saint Menas
Coptic · 6th c. CE · Egypt
Christ stands beside a local Egyptian martyr, his hand familiarly on Menas's shoulder — an unusual intimacy for Christian iconography. A painted wood panel from Bawit monastery.
